title: A Humane Judgment!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: This derived reading copy repairs only source-confirmed or unambiguous OCR spacing and character errors. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged. The long passages quoted from Kalaignar's earlier statements are preserved as quotations rather than condensed.

Yesterday, 21 January 2014, the Supreme Court delivered a humane and historic judgment. In the sandalwood smuggler Veerappan case, seven people were sentenced to five years' imprisonment and another seven to life imprisonment. They appealed against these sentences in the Supreme Court. The Court enhanced the life sentences imposed on A. Simon, M. Bilavendran, J. Gnanaprakasam and Meesai Madaiyan to death sentences. All four submitted mercy petitions to the President of India. After a delay, those petitions were rejected. Because of the delay in deciding their mercy petitions, they filed a writ petition in the Supreme Court seeking commutation of their sentences, as well as a curative petition asking that the case be heard again. The Supreme Court dismissed the curative petition. Chief Justice Sathasivam then said that it was neither proper nor supported by precedent to rehear a case after it had already been heard and decided.

Meanwhile, the Supreme Court has now delivered its judgment on the writ petition filed by the four convicts. It has commuted their death sentences to life imprisonment. Chief Justice Sathasivam and Justice Ranjan Gogoi delivered the judgment.

Across the country, fifteen prisoners remain under sentence of death without execution even after the President has rejected their mercy petitions. The mercy petitions of fourteen other death-row prisoners are under the President's consideration. The present judgment creates the possibility that the sentences of other such prisoners may also be commuted on the ground of delay in deciding their mercy petitions. It is said that this list may include Perarivalan, Santhan and Murugan, the three men sentenced to death in the Rajiv Gandhi assassination case.

Many, including the DMK, have been calling for the release of these three. A report published in The Hindu on 25 July 2013 stated: "A large section of India's voters - constituting the dominant view - is in favour of abolishing the death penalty, as per the CSDS survey." In other words, a survey found that the majority view among Indian voters favoured abolishing the death penalty. Citing this finding, I had appealed through Murasoli for the release of the three men sentenced to death.

In 1993, a car-bomb attack was carried out in Delhi in an attempt to kill Congress leader M. S. Bitta. Bitta survived, but nine people were killed. Devender Pal Singh Bhullar, who carried out the attack, escaped to Germany. After Bhullar was extradited from Germany in 1995, a trial court sentenced him to death in 2001. The Delhi Sessions Court confirmed that sentence in 2002. In the meantime, Bhullar developed a mental illness. In 2003, he submitted a mercy petition to the President seeking release on compassionate grounds. In 2011, then President Pratibha Patil rejected it.

Because there had been a delay in deciding his mercy petition, Bhullar filed a petition in the Supreme Court in September 2011 seeking commutation of his death sentence. That petition was rejected on 12 April 2013. The decision sent a shock through Punjab. Some human-rights organisations demanded that the mentally ill Bhullar should not be executed. The Shiromani Akali Dal, the ruling party in Punjab, strongly supported this position. Punjab Chief Minister Parkash Singh Badal and his son, Deputy Chief Minister Sukhbir Singh, met Prime Minister Manmohan Singh and Union Home Minister Sushilkumar Shinde in person and urged them to show mercy to Bhullar. They said the issue had created a volatile situation in Punjab and asked that mercy be shown to Bhullar, thereby helping restore peace in the State.

When reporters asked Union Home Minister Shinde about this, he said: "The Punjab Chief Minister's request will be considered." Referring to this, I expressed my anguish: "From the Chief Minister to the people of that State, everyone speaks with one voice for commuting the death sentence of a person from Punjab to life imprisonment. But what is the situation in Tamil Nadu?"
That was not all. In a statement I issued on 29 August 2011, I wrote:

"There is hardly anyone who has not expressed the view that the death penalty, which is contrary to human rights and humanitarianism, is unnecessary and could be abolished by every country. Rather than impose the maximum punishment of death on a person accused of murder, it should be commuted to life imprisonment. A prisoner spending the remainder of his life thinking repeatedly of the crime and grieving within himself would serve a greater purpose than executing him. If a man has committed murder, his suffering and remorse throughout his life would not only be harsher than death; it would also give him the opportunity and the path to reform his life. The deeply moving letters we wrote to the Union Government, and the appeals we personally placed before the Prime Minister and all the leaders of India, are known to everyone.

"I plead that the lives of Santhan, Perarivalan and Murugan, who are said to be facing the gallows, be saved. Even if the young leader Rajiv Gandhi himself were alive, in this situation he would have respected the voice of true Tamils and, in keeping with Anna's golden maxim, 'Let us forget; let us forgive,' that great man would have saved the lives of these three. If the death sentence were commuted, recognising that, if they truly committed the offence, their imprisonment for more than twenty years in a state of remorse is itself greater than a life sentence, the Tamil people would wholeheartedly praise and rejoice in this humanitarian act. I therefore appeal again to the Union and State Governments to save these three from the grip of death. I make this appeal also as one who knows what pure lives those released from the gallows went on to lead, and as one who was among those responsible for their release."
Later, in another statement I issued on the same question, I said:

"When the Assembly adopted a resolution seeking the release of these three, Jayalalithaa said in her speech that neither the State Chief Minister nor the Governor had authority in the matter under Article 161 of the Constitution. To this day, Jayalalithaa has repeatedly maintained the same position, namely that the State Chief Minister has no authority in this matter. Yet in the statement she read under Rule 110 in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 30 August 2011, she said: 'Accepting the decision of the Cabinet headed by Karunanidhi, His Excellency the Governor gave his approval on 21 April 2000.' It is by precisely the same procedure that I and several other parties in Tamil Nadu have continuously urged the Tamil Nadu Government and Jayalalithaa, if they genuinely care about saving the lives of these three, to pass a resolution in the State Cabinet and send it to the Governor for approval.

"Similarly, we have continued to appeal that mercy be shown to the four men accused of being Veerappan's associates and sentenced to death, and that their death sentences be commuted to life imprisonment.

"The Mysore TADA court that tried Gnanaprakasam, Simon, Meesai Madaiyan and Bilavendran sentenced the four only to life imprisonment. It was because an appeal was filed against that judgment in the Supreme Court that the Court, in 2004, converted their life sentences into death sentences. Nine years have passed since that judgment, and the death sentences have not been carried out. In the meantime, all four submitted mercy petitions to the President of India. After keeping those petitions pending for a long time, the President rejected them on 11 February 2013.

"On behalf of the four, a petition was filed in the Supreme Court on 16 February 2013 opposing the death sentences and seeking a stay on their execution. The petition also sought commutation of their death sentences to life imprisonment. Their lawyers, Colin Gonsalves and Samik Narain, went to the residence of Chief Justice Altamas Kabir to submit the petition and requested an immediate hearing. The Supreme Court has deferred its decision concerning these four.

"When the United Nations put forward a resolution against the death penalty in 2007, 104 countries supported and signed it. Only 39 countries, including India, voted against it. Following the UN resolution, 90 per cent of the world's countries have abolished the death penalty. Should India not join that majority?

"Those in positions of responsibility must bear in mind that even if the guilty escape, the innocent must never be punished. When Bhullar's sentence is reconsidered, the death sentences of these four, and those of Perarivalan, Santhan and Murugan, should also be considered together and commuted. More generally, I once again urge that the death penalty itself be abolished altogether, and I appeal to the Union and State Governments to find a way to achieve this."

Although I continued to make such appeals, no response came from either the Union or State Government. Now the Supreme Court has delivered this historic judgment in a manner that accepts the deeply held feelings of people like me.

At this moment, I convey my happiness to those who will benefit from this judgment and to their families. On behalf of the people of Tamil Nadu, I also thank the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court who delivered it, and the other judge who joined him in doing so.
With affection,
M. K.
23 January 2014
